one_line_takeaway: By moving the backdoor trigger for semantic-communication image reconstruction from the input image into physical-layer wireless channel statistics (noise power or fading gain), CT-BA achieves near-100% attack success with strong stealth and cross-architecture, cross-fading, and cross-compression robustness, evading five standard backdoor defenses designed for input-domain triggers, while remaining fragile under federated/collaborative training and multi-user broadcast settings.
key_finding: On CIFAR-10/MNIST/ImageNet across R=1/4,1/6,1/12 (Table I, backdoor trained/tested at SNR=-15dB, main task at SNR=15dB), CT-BA achieves ASR of 100.0% on CIFAR-10 and MNIST at all three bandwidth ratios and 99.24-99.82% on ImageNet, while PSNRm stays close to the benign-model PSNRc (e.g., CIFAR-10 R=1/4: PSNRm=46.65dB vs PSNRc=46.36dB) and AEVC tracks CA closely (max discrepancy CA-AEVC <=1.01% on ImageNet R=1/12). Even a 1% poisoning ratio yields ASR>99.99% with PSNRb exceeding PSNRm by 17.92dB (Table II). Trigger specificity was confirmed: sweeping AWGN test SNR from -30 to 15dB, ASR for an n-triggered model stayed below 10% outside its target SNR (peak PSNRb only 18.24dB at -6dB SNR) while the dedicated trigger condition produces near-perfect activation, showing the backdoor is not spuriously triggered by ambient noise. Robustness across Rayleigh, Nakagami-m (m=0.6), and Rician (K=5) fading channels was strong (ASR 99.66-99.93%, Table III) and largely preserved under channel-state missing rate degradation (Fig. 8) and under model pruning + INT8 quantization (Fig. 9, ASR stayed near 100% even under joint pruning+quantization, though PSNRb degraded with pruning amount). Comparing against input-domain WaNet/BadNets triggers ported onto BDJSCC (Table IV, poisoning ratio 0.01, R=1/6): CT-BA achieved PSNRb=34.09dB and ASR=100% versus BadNets (PSNRb=20.28dB, ASR=93.31%) and WaNets (PSNRb=20.21dB, ASR=93.32%), with tighter PSNRm/PSNRc and CA/AEVC alignment, i.e. the channel-domain trigger outperformed input-domain triggers on both effectiveness and stealth in this comparison. Generalization across four SemCom architectures (Table V, CIFAR-10, R=1/6) showed ASR=100.0% for BDJSCC, ADJSCC, JSCCOFDM, and VIT-MIMO, including systems with built-in physical-layer processing (OFDM equalization, SVD-based MIMO sub-channels), indicating the attack is not specific to the ViT-based victim architecture. For the proposed noise-titration defense, the authors report (qualitatively, via titration curves in Fig. 11/12 rather than a summary detection-accuracy table in the excerpted sections) that backdoored models show a sharp confidence rise at a threshold noise variance while benign models show no structured response, and select chi=0.8 as an operating threshold.
core_contribution: The paper proposes CT-BA (Channel-Triggered Backdoor Attack), a backdoor attack on end-to-end deep-learning joint source-channel coding (JSCC) semantic communication (SemCom) systems for image transmission. Unlike prior SemCom backdoor work (BASS, CSBA) that embeds triggers into the input image, CT-BA moves the trigger into the physical wireless channel itself: the adversary conditions the backdoor on channel statistics -- either the channel noise power (n-trigger) or the channel gain/fading distribution (H-trigger) -- so the backdoor activates automatically when the transmitted signal passes through a specific, attacker-chosen channel condition, with no input manipulation and no active adversary participation required at inference time. The attacker is assumed to control model training (outsourced-model threat model), poisoning a fraction of training batches by routing a subset of encoded symbols through a 'backdoor channel' with the trigger parameters while the rest go through the normal channel, jointly optimizing a combined loss (main task + backdoor task, Eq. 12). The authors also propose and evaluate a candidate defense: 'noise titration,' which injects noise of increasing variance into the decoder input at inference time and monitors the classifier confidence response curve to distinguish a backdoored model (sharp, threshold-triggered high-confidence reconstruction of a fixed target) from a benign one (no structured response).
threat_model: Outsourced/supply-chain training threat model, consistent with standard backdoor-attack literature (the paper explicitly cites WaNet and related work for this framing): the attacker has full control over the training process and the training dataset of the SemCom encoder-decoder pair, then delivers the backdoored model to the victim, who deploys it for wireless image transmission. At inference, the trigger is not an input perturbation but a physical-layer channel condition (specific noise power sigma_a for the n-trigger, or a specific channel-gain/fading distribution such as Rayleigh for the H-trigger). Activation can be passive/automatic (e.g., a drone naturally transitioning from line-of-sight to a dense urban multipath environment) or actively induced by an adversary with a jammer that raises the noise floor to a target power level. The paper explicitly notes the attacker is assumed to have public/measurable knowledge of the target channel's noise statistics and the victim system's transmission power -- a 'standard capability assumed for adversaries in wireless environments' per the paper's own characterization.
limitations: The paper's own Section VI (Limitations and Future Works) states: (1) CT-BA's persistence is undermined under collaborative/federated training paradigms increasingly used in SemCom deployments, because global model aggregation and multi-round iterative averaging tend to dilute or 'forget' a single malicious participant's injected trigger, posing a distinct robustness challenge not solved in this paper; (2) in multi-user/broadcast SemCom scenarios, trigger specificity breaks down -- a trigger intended for one victim may be inadvertently activated by other users experiencing similar channel statistics, and the paper only speculates that higher-dimensional triggers could help, without demonstrating a solution; managing the stealthiness/robustness trade-off in such dynamic, multi-interferer networks is described as 'a critical hurdle' left open. Additionally, all evaluation is in simulation (PyTorch channel models), with no results shown on physical hardware/software-defined-radio testbeds, and the proposed noise-titration defense is presented as a discussion/analysis rather than a fully quantified detection benchmark (no reported detection accuracy/AUC against adaptive attackers who might vary poisoning ratio or trigger design specifically to evade titration).

datasets_used: MNIST (70,000 28x28 grayscale images, 10 classes, 60k train/10k test)
ImageNet subset (60,000 train / 50,000 test images from the full >14M-image, 1000-class ImageNet)
quantitative_results: Table I: ASR=100.0% on CIFAR-10 and MNIST at R=1/4,1/6,1/12; ASR=99.24-99.82% on ImageNet at the same ratios (backdoor trained/tested at SNR=-15dB, main task at SNR=15dB)
Table I: CIFAR-10 R=1/4 PSNRm=46.65dB vs PSNRc=46.36dB; ImageNet R=1/12 PSNRm=27.05dB vs PSNRc=27.65dB
Table II: at P=1% poisoning ratio, ASR>99.99% and PSNRb exceeds PSNRm by 17.92dB

Trigger specificity sweep (Fig.7): AEVC rises from 9.9% to 88.86% as SNR increases, while ASR stays below 10% across the whole AWGN SNR sweep away from the target trigger condition, and PSNRb peaks at only 18.24dB (at -6dB SNR)
Table III (fading channels, CIFAR-10, ViT-JSCC): ASR=99.89% (Rayleigh), 99.93% (Nakagami-m, m=0.6), 99.66% (Rician, K=5)
Table IV (BDJSCC, R=1/6, poisoning ratio 0.01): CT-BA PSNRb=34.09dB/ASR=100% vs BadNets PSNRb=20.28dB/ASR=93.31% vs WaNets PSNRb=20.21dB/ASR=93.32%
Table V (cross-architecture, CIFAR-10, R=1/6): ASR=100.0% for all of BDJSCC, ADJSCC, JSCCOFDM, VIT-MIMO
Model compression (Fig.9): ASR remains near 100% under combined L1-unstructured pruning and INT8 quantization on ImageNet, though PSNRb declines as pruning amount increases from 0.0 to 0.4
baselines_compared: Benign (non-backdoored) model trained identically per configuration, used as PSNRc/CA reference in every table
BadNets (visible patch-based input trigger, ported to BDJSCC)
WaNet (elastic-warping imperceptible input trigger, ported to BDJSCC)
